plainMeaning: Lord Mahāvīra's teaching is forever victorious, because it shows the way to liberation, explains the whole of reality, and sweeps away every false belief.
recitation: nivvui-paha-sāsaṇayaṁ, jayai sayā savva-bhāva-desaṇayaṁ; kusamaya-maya-ṇāsaṇayaṁ, jiṇiṁda-vara-vīra-sāsaṇayaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: the path of/to liberation (the road to nirvāṇa-bliss)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit nivvui (< Skt. nirvṛti, 'extinction, repose, final release') + paha (< Skt. patha, 'path'); a tatpuruṣa qualifying śāsana — 'the teaching that is the path to liberation.'
wordByWord.notes: nirvṛti here = mokṣa/nirvāṇa, the soul's release from saṁsāra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sāsaṇa (< Skt. śāsana, 'rule, instruction, dispensation') + svārthika -aya suffix (no change of sense) + acc./nom. -aṁ; the rhyme-word that ends three of the four pādas.
wordByWord.notes: First of two occurrences; the -aya is a non-meaning-bearing taddhita that gives the verse its -ayaṁ rhyme.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jayai (< Skt. jayati, √ji, 'to conquer, prevail'); 3rd-sg. present — the hymn's sealing 'jaya!'
wordByWord.notes: Echoes the jayai-refrain of the sūtra's opening maṅgala, closing the hymn where it began.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva (< Skt. sarva, 'all'); first member of the compound savva-bhāva-desaṇa.
Prakrit/Skt. bhāva (√bhū, 'to be'); here the full range of substances and modes (dravya-paryāya) that the omniscient teaching expounds.
wordByWord.notes: savva-bhāva = the totality of reality grasped in kevalajñāna.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit desaṇā (< Skt. deśanā, √diś, 'to point out, teach') + svārthika -aya + -aṁ; 'savva-bhāva-desaṇa' = 'that which sets forth all existents.'
Prakrit ku- ('bad, wrong') + samaya ('doctrine, tenet') < Skt. ku-samaya; a pejorative for mithyā-darśana / rival ekānta views.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary's śāsana is the true samaya; kusamaya is its opposite.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit maya (< Skt. mada, 'conceit, intoxication'; intervocalic d→y); compound kusamaya-maya = 'the arrogance of false creeds.'
wordByWord.notes: Read here as mada ('conceit'); some reciters take maya as māyā ('delusion') — see notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ṇāsaṇa (< Skt. nāśana, √naś caus., 'to destroy') + svārthika -aya + -aṁ; initial n→ṇ per Prakrit phonology.
wordByWord.notes: kusamaya-maya-ṇāsaṇa = 'destroyer of the conceit of false doctrine.'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jiṇa ('Jina, conqueror') + iṁda (< Skt. indra, 'lord, chief') = jinendra; first member of the final epithet-compound.
Prakrit/Skt. vīra ('hero, valiant one'); the abbreviated name of the 24th Tīrthaṅkara.
wordByWord.notes: Names the teaching's source: jiṇiṁda-vara-vīra-sāsaṇa = 'the dispensation of the supreme Jinendra Vīra.'

wordByWord.etymology: Repetition of token 2; here the head-noun of the whole sentence — the subject that 'ever triumphs.'
wordByWord.notes: Second occurrence; the three preceding -ayaṁ epithets all qualify this final sāsaṇayaṁ.

literalTranslation: Ever-victorious is the teaching that is the path to liberation — the teaching that expounds all existents, the destroyer of the conceit of false doctrines — the teaching of the supreme Lord-of-Jinas, Vīra.
idiomaticTranslation: Forever triumphant is the dispensation of the supreme Lord Jina, Vīra: it is the very road to liberation, it lays bare the whole of reality, and it shatters the arrogance of every false creed — may that teaching ever prevail!

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The hymn closes by lifting its gaze from those who carry the teaching back to the teaching itself. Where v.23 named the eleven Gaṇadharas — the human channels of Vīra's word — v.24 acclaims that word's own perpetual triumph, sealing the sajjhāy with the same jayai ('it conquers!') that opened the Nandī maṅgala. The craft is a fourfold rhyme: three epithet-compounds (sāsaṇayaṁ, desaṇayaṁ, ṇāsaṇayaṁ) all converge on a fourth, the head-word sāsaṇayaṁ, so the ear hears 'teaching… teaching… teaching' hammered home. Each epithet states one office of the śāsana. As nivvui-paha-sāsaṇa it is soteriological — the very road to mokṣa, not merely a description of it. As savva-bhāva-desaṇa it is epistemic — the omniscient disclosure of every substance and mode, the fruit of kevala-jñāna. As kusamaya-maya-ṇāsaṇa it is polemical — it does not merely differ from rival doctrines but dissolves their mada, their self-intoxicated conceit. Only after these three functions are stated is the source named: jiṇiṁda-vara-vīra, 'the supreme Lord Jina, Vīra.' The verse thus moves liberation → omniscience → refutation → author, and the single finite verb jayai sayā ('ever triumphs') governs the whole — a benediction the reciter pronounces over the entire path just traversed.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The three epithets compress the Jain self-understanding of its own teaching. Nivvui-paha answers to the ratnatraya as mokṣa-mārga (Tattvārtha-sūtra 1.1): the śāsana is identical with the path of right faith, knowledge and conduct, not an external map of it. Savva-bhāva-desaṇa rests on omniscience and anekānta: a teaching grounded in kevala-jñāna can expound the totality of dravyas and paryāyas, which a partial (ekānta) view cannot. Kusamaya-maya-ṇāsaṇa frames false doctrine's defect not as mere error but as mada — the conceited absolutizing of one standpoint that syādvāda deflates. The triad therefore claims completeness (it covers all reality), efficacy (it reaches liberation), and corrective power (it cures dogmatic pride).
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this closing gāthā, the practitioner converts study into allegiance: having praised Vīra, the Saṅgha and the Gaṇadharas, one now affirms confidence in the teaching that unites them, pronouncing 'jaya' as a quiet vow to walk the nivvui-paha. The line also tempers the polemic — what is to be destroyed is mada, conceit, not persons — a useful check on the reciter's own dogmatic pride.

crossReferences.text: Nandī-sūtra opening maṅgala — 'jayaï… mahāvīro' (the jayai-refrain this verse echoes to close the hymn)

sources.title: Nivvui / Nivvuī (Prakrit, < Skt. nirvṛti)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of nivvui as nirvṛti — liberation/final release, for the compound nivvui-paha

sources.title: Kusamaya, Kusa-maya (heterodox doctrine)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of kusamaya as a heterodox/false religious doctrine (Kannada lexicon entry)

sources.title: Deśanā / Desana (exposition, teaching)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of desaṇa(yaṁ) as deśanā, 'expounding,' for savva-bhāva-desaṇa

sources.title: Śāsana / Sasana (rule, dispensation, teaching)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of sāsaṇa(yaṁ) as śāsana, the Jina's teaching/dispensation

notes: The verse is an epithet-stacked closing benediction; the head-noun is the second sāsaṇayaṁ ('teaching'), with the verb jayai sayā ('ever triumphs') and the three preceding -ayaṁ compounds all qualifying it. The taddhita -aya in sāsaṇaya/desaṇaya/ṇāsaṇaya is svārthika (sense-neutral), inserted for the four-pāda rhyme; I have not assigned it independent meaning. Chief ambiguity: માય/મય 'maya' — read here as mada ('conceit, intoxication') of false doctrine, the more common parsing; a minority reading takes it as māyā ('delusion/deceit'), giving 'destroyer of the deceit of false doctrine.' One web source mis-segmented 'paha' (path) as 'passions' and 'sayā' (always) as 'self' — both rejected on standard Prakrit grounds. Orthography (anusvāras, ણ, doubled conjuncts) follows the printed book verbatim and is not normalized. The closing jayai deliberately mirrors the Nandī maṅgala's opening jayai-litany.
